title: MicroCT scanning and analysis in the zebrafish skeleton
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"uk0ex","text":"Zebrafish and other small laboratory fish have emerged as important model systems for bone biomedical research. Micro-computed tomography (microCT) is a powerful approach to non-destructively visualize and quantify bone morphology, microarchitecture, and mineral density. While consensus methods for microCT scanning analysis have been established in other vertebrates including small rodents, the small size of zebrafish, their different anatomy, and their amenability to rapid-throughput and high-content approaches call for unique considerations. In this chapter, we describe key considerations for performing microCT scans in the zebrafish skeleton. We also describe different approaches for sample mounting, scan acquisition, and analysis that can be used depending on experimental objectives and outcomes. Finally, we describe a step-by-step protocol for microCT scanning and analysis of adult zebrafish using FishCuT, resulting in hundreds of descriptors of vertebral morphology and mineral density.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"rsjzg","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"bgqmk","text":"Zebrafish (Danio rerio) have emerged as a powerful model for bone biomedical research [1-5]. MicroCT imaging allows for non-destructive imaging and analysis of the skeleton and other mineralized structures. Whereas approaches and guidelines for microCT scanning and analysis have been well established for small rodents [6], microCT scanning and analysis pipelines for zebrafish are more recent and thus are rapidly evolving [7-11]. The inability to directly apply microCT methods optimized for small rodents to zebrafish is due in part to their smaller size, different anatomy, and their amenability to rapid-throughput and high-content approaches. For instance, due to their reduced labor, cost, and space requirements, it is often feasible for individual labs to perform forward and reverse genetic screens in zebrafish. Moreover, the small size of zebrafish facilitates approaches for phenomics—in-depth skeletal phenotyping at a large number of skeletal sites. It has previously been shown that, compared to scanning and analyzing a single vertebra at high resolution, microCT-based phenomics can increase sensitivity in detecting variants of smaller effect size [7]. Thus, there is a need for microCT scanning and analysis protocols that facilitate both (i) “traditional” analyses of single bones, and (ii) higher throughput scanning and analysis protocols for rapid-throughput and high-content screening approaches.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":11,"length":11}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"tvcym","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"idynr","text":"Our objectives in this chapter are to provide (i) a concise discussion of key considerations when devising microCT scanning and analysis protocols for zebrafish, (ii) a flexible protocol for sample preparation and scanning that can be tailored for both “traditional” analyses of single bones in small groups of fish, as well as higher throughput, high-content studies, and (iii) steps for semi-automatic segmentation and analysis of vertebrae using FishCuT to quantify ~200 descriptors of vertebral morphology and mineral density per fish (10 measures x 20 vertebrae) [7, 12, 13](Fig 1). Some caveats should be noted. First, this chapter focuses on the use of FishCuT, a custom software program written with the intention of performing deep phenotypic analysis of the adult spine. However, the analysis of other structures and measures might be desirable. Most commercial microCT systems will come with their own software (e.g., Bruker CT-Analyser (CTAn) and Scanco Medical IPL software) that allows complex regions and volumes of interest to be delineated, which achieves separation for analysis of specific bones or other organs (Fig 2). There is also free, open-source software (e.g., 3D Slicer) for this purpose. Details on the use of some of these applications for the analysis of bones in zebrafish and small rodents have been described in previously published protocols [14, 15]. Second, our discussion of key considerations for microCT scanning and analysis is not meant to be exhaustive as further details can be attained from published resources [6, 16]. Thirdly, we focus our chapter on adult zebrafish (\u003e3 months post fertilization), since skeleton at this age is sufficiently mineralized to be resolved by commercial microCT systems [7]. We also focus on ex vivo scans since in vivo microCT scanning remains relatively uncommon due to the challenges with long-term anesthesia in the adult zebrafish [17]. Finally, this protocol was developed using a Scanco vivaCT40 scanner. While specific scanning procedures will vary for different applications and machines, the considerations discussed in this chapter are general and many aspects of the protocol can be adapted to other systems.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":1768,"length":7},{"style":"italic","offset":1788,"length":7}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"zomor","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"wt1ln","text":"1.1 General considerations","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":3},{"style":"bold","offset":4,"length":22}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"amnbt","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"6faub","text":"1.1.1 Sample storage","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":5},{"style":"bold","offset":6,"length":14}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"muye4","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"xx20d","text":"MicroCT studies of bone can be conducted in samples that were frozen, fixed, or freshly culled, with the method chosen based on the application and compatibility with downstream analyses. In choosing the storage method, it is important to consider how it may affect tissue hydration status. Ideally, for bone mineral density (BMD) measurement, the sample would be fully hydrated similar to the in vivo state. This is because bone mineral density (BMD) measures attenuation-equivalent hydroxyapatite concentration in a mixture of bone and soft tissue. Removal of a sample from storage in alcohol will always result in dehydration of the sample, which can lead to altered attenuation values. Soft tissue is particularly prone to detrimental effects arising from dehydration. In the worst cases, soft tissue regions may develop air in them, resulting in severely compromised – and even negative – BMD values. To minimize complications arising from sample dehydration in soft tissue, one approach is to measure “tissue mineral density” (TMD) where the density measurement is restricted to only the mineralized tissue volume. TMD measurement from microCT scans can be done using binary masking – employing a bone threshold binary image as the volume of interest (VOI) mask within which density measurement is restricted. Both TMD and bone mineral content (BMC) can be measured in this way.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":394,"length":8}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"l19kp","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"wfuda","text":"It is also important to consider how storage may affect mineral status. Zebrafish can be fixed and stored briefly in 10% neutral buffered formalin or 4% paraformaldehyde (PFA) prior to storage for microCT imaging. Caution should be used if storing in formalin – even buffered – for more than a few days, as formaldehyde tends to oxidize into methanoic acid, which can lead to acid demineralization of bone. One alternative approach is to fix samples in 4% PFA for 24h, followed by washes in serial ethanol solutions (25%, 50% and 70%) for 1h each. Samples can then be stored long-term in 70% ethanol at 4˚C. The protocol described below utilizes frozen storage. It should be recognized that this method is not without some risk of freeze-drying and dehydration. Regardless of the storage method used, the same method should be used for both controls and experimental animals in all cases.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"gkpla","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ys03u","text":"1.1.2 Scanning medium during scanning","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":37}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ao5ek","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4kx7r","text":"Animals can be scanned in a variety of mediums including air or liquid. Efforts to maintain sample hydration during microCT scanning should be considered, as dehydration during scanning can result in sample movement and subsequent motion artifacts. If it is desired to scan a sample in alcohol (which will avoid soft tissue dehydration due to its removal from the medium), the sample can be physically stabilized (e.g., in foam) so that it won’t move during the scan. However, the liquid around the sample will add noise and reduce the quality of the microCT reconstructed image. Scanning animals in air is useful for some situations. This includes cases where automatic segmentation of soft tissue from the surrounding medium is necessary, such as lean mass quantification, and segmentation of individual fish from multiplexed (bulk) microCT scans [7]. The same scanning medium should be used across an entire experiment. The protocol below describes a paper towel-based method to suspend the fish within the sample holder, while providing an optional hydration step to help maintain tissue hydration during scanning if desired.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"1j8bi","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"sgwy1","text":"1.1.3 Voxel size and field of view","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":34}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"mhr01","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"tcaay","text":"Another key consideration is voxel size and its relationship to field of view and time of scan acquisition. Ideally, the smallest achievable voxel size would be used in all cases, however, a smaller field of view and longer scan duration are a consequence of smaller voxel sizes. Since zebrafish are amenable to large-scale approaches such as forward or reverse genetic screens, scanning throughput is important to consider. Moreover, as mentioned before, zebrafish are amenable to whole-body phenotyping which can be advantageous. Depending on the model of microCT scanner employed, individual zebrafish may require a single scan or multiple connected scans, and thus, longer acquisition times, to image the whole length. This is partly related to the pixel format of the camera employed, and whether the camera is of the faster “flat panel” CMOS type or of the CCD or compact scientific CMOS type, which are slower due to smaller pixels, but provide the possibility of higher spatial resolution. For example, the Bruker SkyScan 1275, equipped with a flat panel camera with a 1944 x 1536 (horizontal and vertical respectively) pixel format, can scan a whole adult zebrafish at 21 mm voxel size with a single scan rotation. At the same nominal resolution, the Scanco vivaCT40 typically requires three connected scans to image the whole zebrafish length. Thus, the balance between voxel size, field of view, and throughput must be carefully considered. Other considerations include equipment depreciation with chronic x-ray degradation of scanner components, and further downstream, the need for larger data storage and more powerful computers for analysis. For the protocol described below, we provide scan settings for both 10.5 and 21 mm voxel sizes and describe analyses of zebrafish scanned with the latter nominal resolution.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"5sywq","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3sxmv","text":"1.1.4 Multiplexing","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":18}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"u5hip","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"saf7f","text":"A final consideration is whether to multiplex by scanning multiple fish at the same time to increase throughput [7]. This reduces the effective scan acquisition time per animal by fourfold. While multiplexed scanning increases throughput, there are potential compromises regarding image quality. For example, this can cause mutual x-ray shadowing between fish which increases both image noise and beam hardening artefacts, potentially reducing the quality of resultant quantitative data. To minimize the potential for fish in the center of the scan to have lower density measurements due to cupping artifacts caused by beam hardening, fish are positioned relatively equidistant from the scan center (Hur, Gistelinck et al. 2017). Complications arising from batch effects due to density between groups can be reduced by including both controls and mutants in the same bulk scan. Finally, it is important to note that in many commercial desktop microCT scanners, automated batch scanning of individual fish is possible. This can be achieved by mounting zebrafish serially in a plastic tube so that up to 4 or 5 fish can be mounted for scanning in a single tube for a batch run. Alternatively, fully automated robotic multi-position sample changers (Fig 3) are available with many commercial microCT scanners which facilitate large batch scans while scanning fish individually. While batch scanning approaches do not in and of themselves reduce scan acquisition time per animal, in practice they can help to reduce time and labor needed to perform scanning of large numbers of animals by eliminating the need for operators to change samples after each scan. In the protocol described below, we provide details on how to mount individual fish, with an optional step to arrange fish in a 2x2 array format to allow scanning of four fish simultaneously.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"awtlf","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

steps: In this protocol, we assume that there are a relatively large number of fish which need to be scanned over multiple days. As a result, all fish that will be scanned should be prepared and frozen as described below. Multiple 2x2 bundles of fish can be stored in 50mL conical tubes in the -20C freezer until they are ready for scanning (see Note 1).
Begin by following the standard procedure to euthanize the zebrafish you will be scanning.
Dry the countertop where you will be prepping the fish.

Wrap the fish individually in a paper towel before scanning (Fig 4) (see Note 2). This will help keep them separated from each other and centrally positioned within the sample holder. You will need a short strip of paper towel with one end slightly folded over to help wrap the fish. To create this, take a paper towel folded in its natural trifold state and, from the short end, cut a strip that is 2.5 cm wide. Unfold the strip and cut off the end ~0.5 cm past the natural crease of the tri fold. You should now have a short strip of paper towel with ~0.5 cm of one end folded over.
Flip the strip of paper towel over and, using a permanent lab marker, label the opposite end from where your crease is with the ID of your fish. This should be done prior to wrapping the fish.
Cut a 10cm length of Transpore tape. Split the length of tape in half longwise. Cut those pieces into short segments around 1 cm long.

Use a plastic spoon to gently scoop a euthanized fish and lay it on a stack of paper towels. It can be helpful to drill a few small holes into the spoon to enable excess water to drain. Flip the fish over to dry each side. The fish will not be completely dry but this will remove excess water.
Scoop up your fish with the plastic spoon and lay it in the crease of your paper towel strip.

Wrap the fish snugly, without damaging the fish, in the paper towel strip and tape closed with a segment of the Transpore tape. You will want the jaw to be visible, sticking out of the top of the wrap, to help you line the fish up later. Your label should now appear on the outside of the wrapped fish.
Optional: Create fish bundles for multiplexed scanning (Fig 5).

Repeat the above steps for three other fish, such that you have four prepped fish total (Fig 5A). Ideally, choose two controls and two experimental fish for each bundle. Having “experimental” fish along with “control fish” in each scan will mitigate any differences between the experimental and control groups arising from batch effects.

To create a 2x1 bundle, select one control and one experimental fish. Hold the two wrapped fish together so that the jaws of the fish are aligned, with the control fish on the left and the experimental fish on the right (or make a note of which side the fish are on and be consistent). Tape over the top of the two fish with a small piece of the Transpore tape and then gently adjust the fish alignment as needed before taping over the bottom of both with another piece of tape. Repeat with the other two fish (Fig 5B).
Stack the first 2x1 bundle on top of the second 2x1 bundle and secure with a small piece of Transpore tape to make a 2x2 bundle (Fig 5C and 5D).
Individually wrapped fish or 2x2 fish bundles can be stored in 50mL conical tubes in a -20C freezer until ready to scan.
In this protocol, we are using a Scanco vivaCT40. The sample holder is cylindrical in shape and approximately 14 mm in diameter (See Note 3).
Remove the individually wrapped fish or frozen 2x2 fish bundle from the 50mL tube. Let thaw for 10-15 min.

Insert the individually wrapped fish or fish bundle into the sample holder, with the head(s) facing toward you. Place fish into the cylinder prior to mounting onto the base holder. You want the fish to be snug inside so that they will not move when jostled, so you may need to wrap them with another strip of paper towel to make the wrap larger. If needed, push them in further with forceps so that no part is outside of the sample holder (Fig 6B and 6C).
Rotate the sample holder such that the fish dorsoventral axis is perpendicular to the ground, ventral side down (Fig 6D). The sample is now ready to scan.

Optional: Once the wrapped fish is positioned in the sample holder, moisten paper with either a drop of tap water or saline. This will help to keep the sample hydrated while scanning. This is not recommended when performing multiplexed imaging as it is important that fish are maintained in their original positions and wetting can result in excessive movement.
Before using the scanner ensure that the microCT and the radiation indicator light are off.

Load the sample holder. Open the scanner door and slide the sample holder onto the gold “track”. Slide the sample holder all the way in and lock it into place by pulling the lever clockwise and the sample holder is firmly in a locked position. Close the scanner door.
In the scanner software, click on “New” to assign a new sample number. Enter the sample information.
Select the Control file with the desired scan parameters (Note 4).

For medium resolution scans of unstained adult zebrafish, we have used the following parameters: Holder Type: Generic, Any D x L 145 mm Mode: Continuous ROT Evaluation: Default Evaluation Energy/Intensity: 55 kVp, 145 mA, 8 W FOV/Diameter (mm): 21.5 Voxel size (mm): 21 Integration Time: 200 ms Average Data: 1

For high resolution scans of unstained adult zebrafish, we have used the following parameters: Holder Type: Generic, Any D x L 145 mm Mode: Continuous ROT Evaluation: Default Evaluation Energy/Intensity: 55 kVp, 145 mA, 8 W FOV/Diameter (mm): 21.5 Voxel size (mm): 10.5 Integration Time: 200 ms Average Data: 1
Perform the scout-view by selecting “Scout-View”.

Define the scan volume. Select “Reference-line” and you will see a horizontal line appear. Move this line so that it is slightly beyond the tip of the fish’s head and press the ‘shift’ key to freeze it. Continue to press ‘shift’ and move your cursor down to the bottom of the fish. For whole body scans, place the line for the lower limit slightly beyond the end of the tail fin of the fish. As you move the cursor down, you should see the time for the scan in minutes increase. Stop once you have the entire tail in your scan, and then continue to increase the lower limit as much as possible without the scan time increasing any more. Once you have reached this lower limit, click the mouse to set these as limits.
If you are satisfied with the reference lines, you can start the scan. Select Okay/Scan/Start Measurement. Your scan is now in progress.
When your scan is complete, remove the sample from the sample holder. Wrapped fish (individuals or bundles) can be placed back into 50mL conical tubes and stored in a -20C freezer.

We use a custom IPL script to automatically segment fish in the bulk scan and create .DCM files for each fish. The script (MAKE CT DICOM.COM) can be downloaded at the FishCuT Open Science Framework link.
Select ‘Applications’ and then open a DECterm by clicking ‘DECterm’.
Change the directory to where the MAKE CT DICOM.COM file is located.

Run the script by using the @ command along with the number of fish per scan and the scanner-assigned measurement number. For example, if there are 4 fish per scan, and the measurement number is 16354, “@MAKE CT DICOM 4 16354”. The script allows for batch operation for processing of multiple scans. For example, if you would like to process measurement numbers 16354, 16355, 16356, and 16357, you could enter “@MAKE CT DICOM 4 16354 16355 16356 16357” or “@MAKE CT DICOM 4 16354:16357”.

This operation will generate a .ZIP file for each fish. Each will have a name like “C0015050 254 395 CT DCM.zip” that can be used to identify the individual animal within the bulk scan. The first three-digit number (254) in the file name corresponds to the x-axis location of the fish in the bulk scan. The second three-digit number corresponds to the location of the fish in the y-axis. The left, top fish is therefore the fish with the smaller x and y coordinates (Fig 7).
Prior to using FishCuT for the first time, it is recommended you watch the demonstration video which can be viewed at the Open Science Framework link.
Transfer DICOM images to your computer where FishCuT is installed. Open the zip file for the fish you intend to analyze. It is recommended to temporarily save it onto the desktop.
If necessary, rename the folder that appears. This must be the folder with the .DCM images DIRECTLY inside. For example: C0016050 255 395 CT DCM msbl f0 control 1.

Open MATLAB and verify that the FishCuT.m script contains the correct paths to the mij files. Also, add the path to the MIJ java package by running the command “addpath(‘/Applications/Fiji.app/scripts’)”.
In MATLAB, open FishCuT by running the command “FishCuT.” You should see the start window.

Select “Start” and select the .DCM sequence to analyze. Navigate to the folder containing the .DCM images for analysis, click on the first image in the sequence, and hit “Open” (Fig 8A). Note that in the .DCM images, FishCuT assumes that the dorsal aspect of the fish is at the top, and ventral is at the bottom. Moreover, it assumes that the images will be sequentially numbered with the first image being the most rostral, and the last image being most caudal. It is important that this convention be followed for FishCuT to operate correctly.
The program will then ask for image parameters:
Resolution (mm) is the voxel size.

Slope and intercept relate the pixel values to mg HA/ccm density values: mg HA/ccm = x*slope + intercept, where x is the pixel value. These values are reported by the Scanco calibration program (see Note 6). For example: Slope: 281.706 Intercept: -195.402

The threshold multiplier is an empirically-derived value that is multiplied to the threshold value automatically calculated by FishCuT to adjust it in cases when the automatically calculated value is too conservative or stringent. We usually use a value of 0.73. It is important to use the same threshold multiplier across your experiment. See Note 7.
After entering the image parameters, enter “OK”.
FishCuT will read in the .DCM files and perform some initial computations.

FishCuT will show a maximum intensity projection and an ROI outlining the fish that it automatically generated. Visually inspect the images and verify that the program has accurately outlined the entirety of the fish body. The outline does not need to be perfect, but it should encapsulate the entire fish. If you are satisfied, enter “e”. If not, you can manually specify the outline. To do this, enter “m” and use the left mouse button to click around the fish body, generating sequential line segments until reaching the initial tracing point. Once a closed ROI is specified, FishCuT will automatically proceed to the next step (Fig 8B).

FishCuT will then calculate connectivity and generate labelled projections. FishCuT will generate two images. The image with different colors shows the initial connectivity computed by FishCuT. Specifically, it shows the individual connected components, assigns them different colors, and makes a 2D projection for visualization. We will use this image to guide further segmentation steps, with the goal of making “cuts” so that no vertebra is connected to other vertebrae or tissues. The grey-scale image is a maximum intensity projection which you will use to specify ROI for vertebral segmentation. Once you see the message “Enter “s” to segment, or “e” to exit” in the Command Window, you can proceed.

Perform the initial vertebral segmentation. Enter “s” to segment, then enter “v” for vertebral segmentation. On the grey-scale image, use the mouse to draw line segments that define the intervertebral regions. Each line consists of two points: a dorsal point and a ventral point. The dorsal point must always be specified first. See the tutorial located at the Open Science Framework link for guidance on point placement. FishCuT will keep track of how many lines you draw; for example, if you draw 17 lines, it will segment the 16 vertebrae that are flanked by the 17 lines. Segment 16-24 vertebrae by drawing 17-25 lines, starting with the anterior end of the first pre-caudal (rib-bearing vertebra). If you would like to redo a line segment, press “b” to erase the line segment you just made. Once you are finished, press “space”. The program will compute the new connectivity (Fig 8C).

Perform fine segmentation. If the initial segmentation is not sufficient to isolate each vertebra, use fine segmentation to create further “cuts”. Enter “s” to segment, then “f” for fine segmentation. Draw line segments where you would like to cut (see the tutorial located at the Open Science Framework link for an example). Once you are finished, press “space”. The program will compute the new connectivity. Repeat this step until you are satisfied with the connectivity. When you are done, press “e” to exit.

Perform vertebral assignments (Fig 8D). Here, you will assign which connected components belong to which vertebra. Starting with the anterior-most precaudal vertebra (“vertebra 1”), click on the connected component corresponding to the vertebra. Once you click on it, it will disappear from the image. Each vertebra should be composed of three parts: the vertebral body, the neural arch, and the haemal arch or ribs (depending on vertebrae location). If there are additional connected components that comprise vertebra 1, click on these as well. Once all connected components are selected, hit space to proceed to the next vertebra. Repeat for all vertebrae.

After you have numbered all the vertebrae and hit space, FishCuT will generate a heatmap of the data, images of the fish, and a folder containing the data (Fig 8E). If performed correctly, you should see a fish with alternating colors for each vertebra and corresponding ribs/hemal arch and neural arch.
Repeat for all the fish in your experiment.
Download the “FishCuT analysis.R” file from the Open Science Framework link.
Create a folder containing: the .txt files from FishCuT for analysis. The “FishCuT analysis.R” script.

Open the R application by double clicking on the .R file. By opening R in this way, the correct working directory will be specified. Alternatively, specify the working directory by clicking “Misc" and ”Change Working Directory.”

Edit the files names in the dataFiles vector of the R script so that they match the .txt file names being analyzed. The data file names should be entered as arguments separated by commas within the c() function in R, which combines the arguments into a vector.

Change the groupIDs vector so that order and number of the “mutant” and “control” labels correspond to the order and number of the data files in the dataFiles vector. For example, if the first data file is a control, then the first argument within the groupIDs vector should be labeled “control”.
Change the legend labels in the legendLabels vector to match the groups being analyzed.

You can also change the measurements included in the analysis, the vertebrae being analyzed, the p-value which will be considered significant, and whether the y-axis is set to zero by editing the paramsToAnalyze, vertsToAnalyze, and pValueThresh variables within the .R script. For example, to specify which of the 26 available parameters should be analyzed, add the appropriate parameter numbers as arguments within the “paramsToAnalyze” vector.
Save the .R file.
Run the script. You can do this by typing “source(“FishCuT analysis.R”)” in the console and hitting “enter”. Alternatively, you copy and paste your script into the R console and hit “enter”.

The script will generate graphs for the measurements and characteristics of the vertebrae you selected. For each measurement, the script will perform the global test to compute a p-value. Those that are less than the specified p-value will be rendered in the lighter coloring scheme.
Open FIJI.
Drag the desired .DCM folder onto the FIJI window. Click “Yes”. A window will appear with a slide bar, which is used to look at all the slices of the scanned fish.

Select “Image” from the toolbar, followed by “Stacks” and “Reslice”. On the window that appears, make sure “Left” and “Avoid Interpolation” are selected. Check “Rotate 90 degrees” to position the fish horizontally.

Once the resliced image appears, select “Image” in the toolbar, followed by “Stacks” and “Z Project”. In the new “Z Projection” window, select “Max Intensity” in the “Projection Type” options. A maximum intensity projection (MIP) image will appear.
Save the image as a .tif file by selecting “File” from the toolbar followed by “Save As” and ”Tiff”.

Montage of MIPs can also be created in FIJI. First, open all the desired MIP .tif files in the desired order for the montage. Select “Image” from the toolbar, then “Images to Stack”. A window of all the MIPs stacked together will appear. Again select “Image”, then “Make Montage”. On the window that appears, you can choose the number of rows and columns to determine how the montage will appear. Use “1” for “Scale factor” to restore the quality of the image in the montage.

Notes 1. If you only have a few fish to scan, freshly culled animals can be scanned immediately after being prepared using the procedures described. 2. A variety of methods can be used to stabilize the fish. For example, as an alternative to paper towel, you can wrap the fish in a roll of tissue paper, with a width cut to about 50% longer than the fish. Alternatively, embedding in agarose/agar gel is another method of mounting biological tissue for microCT imaging. It achieves high stability which is good for microCT imaging. However, like scanning in alcohol, it has the disadvantage of adding a surrounding medium that will add significant additional noise to microCT images (although agar gel has X-ray attenuation a little less than water). 3. This mounting helps to ensure symmetrical sample placement and rotation during scanning and can allow batch scanning if several zebrafish can be mounted serially in the tube. If samples are mounted in series in a sample tube for batch scanning, take care to note the identity of all samples for correct identification during and after scanning. 4. This protocol was developed on a microCT scanner lacking a filter changer. If available, the use of a 0.25mm Al filter is recommended to help narrow the x-ray photon energy spectrum and reduce beam hardening, improving the accuracy of skeletal densitometry measurement. 5. Segmentation of individual fish and generation of .DCM files can also be performed manually. A protocol for this described in [14]. 6. The Scanco calibration is based on signed short integer pixel values. Moreover, Scanco applies a scaling value to convert between calculated 32-bit float 'linear attenuation coefficient' and stored short integer. In our experience this scaling value is always 4096. Thus, when the detected input type is signed short integer, a scaling of 4096 is automatically applied. The program can also work with unsigned short integers. When this type is detected, the 4096 scaling will be removed. The initial computed connectivity by FishCuT can give an indication of whether threshold adjustment is needed. If many pixels that you would classify as bone are being excluded, use a threshold multiplier <1. If all vertebrae appear connected, this may be due to too low of a threshold and thus you may try using a threshold multiplier >1.

Figure 1. Fig 1. Quantification bone morphology and density in the adult zebrafish skeleton via microCT. Shown are sample outputs for the protocol described in this chapter. (A-J) Phenotypic measures computed by FishCuT, indicated by the graph title with units for y axis. Measures are plotted as a function of vertebra along the spine. Measures with p&lt;0.05 in the global test are rendered in a lighter colorscheme. Cent, centrum; Haem, haemal arch; Neur, neural arch; Vol, volume; TMD, tissue mineral density; Th, thickness; Le, length. (K) Maximum intensity projections of a control and mutant fish. Fig 1 adapts portions of Fig 7 from the following paper: Watson CJ, Tang WJ, Rojas MF, Fiedler IAK, Morfin Montes de Oca E, Cronrath AR, et al. (2022) wnt16 regulates spine and muscle morphogenesis through parallel signals from notochord and dermomyotome. PLoS Genet 18(11): e1010496. The paper of Watson et al., which was published in PLoS Genetics, applies the Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY) license ( https://journals.plos.org/plosone/s/licenses-and-copyright ). Figure 2. Fig 2. Segmentation and visualization of bones using Bruker software. Shown are images for a (A) fish head and mandible and (B) vertebra. In this approach, Bruker CT-Analyser (CTAn) was used to draw successive ROI shapes by freehand (left images). These shapes are interpolated to create complex volumes of interest for subsequent thresholding, segmentation, 3D morphometric/densitometric analysis, and visualization (right images). The fish head and mandible images were generated by Marching Cubes surface rendering in Bruker CT-Volume (CTVol) while the vertebra 3D image was generated by Bruker CT-Voxel (CTVox) software. Figure 3. Fig 3. Fully automated robotic multi-position sample changers facilitate large batch scans. Shown are robo-sample changers for the (A) Bruker SkyScan 1272 and (B) Scanco CT100 scanners. These changers allow for multiple samples to be scanned in a batch without intervention. Figure 4. Fig. 4. Method for wrapping zebrafish in paper towel. (A) Top: Schematic demonstrating the procedure for wrapping zebrafish in the paper towel. (B) Image of a cut tri-fold paper towel prepared for wrapping. (C) Image of a wrapped zebrafish in labeled paper towel. Figure 5. Fig. 5. Method for bundling zebrafish. (A) Single wrapped fish. (B) A 2x1 bundle created by taping wrapped fish side by side, ensuring the dorsal-ventral axis is as vertical as possible. (C) A 2x2 bundle created by stacking 2x1 bundles on top of each other. (D) Image demonstrating appropriate alignment of fish in the bundle. Figure 6. Fig. 6. Placement of zebrafish into the sample holder. (A) Cylindrical sample holder (left) and base holder (right). (B) Insertion of a 2x1 bundle into the sample holder. Note the additional paper towel wrapped around the 2x1 bundle to stabilize the fish within the holder. (C) Insertion of a 2x2 bundle into the sample holder. (D) Image of a 2x2 bundle in the sample holder with improper orientation. In this case, the sample holder should be rotated counterclockwise such that the dorsoventral axis is vertical. Figure 7. Fig 7. Identification of samples following automatic segmentation of individual fish from the bulk scan. Each fish will automatically be assigned a file name similar to “C0015050 275 206 CT DCM.zip” that can be used to identify the individual animal within the bulk scan. The first three-digit number (blue) in the file name corresponds to the x-axis location of the fish in the bulk scan, whereas the second three-digit number (red) corresponds to the location of the fish in the y-axis. In this convention, it is assumed that the origin is at the top left, the dorsal side is up, and the rostral side is closest such that the fish are “facing” you. Made using Biorender.com. Figure 8. Fig 8. Segmentation using FishCuT. (A) Initiation of the FishCuT script in MatLab. Check parameters before hitting OK. (B) Max Intensity Projection of a fish and subsequent output of fish skeleton. (C) Example of projected segmentation result – individual vertebrae should be assigned different colors. (D) Example of vertebral assignments. (E) FishCuT results include a projection of the final vertebral segmentation, a heatmap of the data, and a folder containing the data within the folder of .DCM images. Figure 9. Fig 9. Generation of maximum intensity projections using FIJI.
